vignette: A 55-year-old male with no significant medical history asks about age-appropriate cancer screenings. He is a never-smoker. Which screening is recommended for him?

options.text: Low-dose CT chest for lung cancer

explanation.whyCorrect: CRC screening starts at 45. Lung cancer screening is for smokers ≥20 pack-years.

whyWrong.A: Lung cancer screening is only for heavy smokers.
whyWrong.D: AAA screening is for males 65-75 who ever smoked.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Screenings at 50: CRC (colonoscopy), Lung (if smoker), Breast (mammogram if female).

vignette: A 78-year-old male with advanced dementia is admitted with aspiration pneumonia. His daughter (healthcare proxy) requests 'everything possible be done.' The medical team believes this would be futile. What is the BEST approach?

options.text: Follow the daughter's wishes and intubate if needed
Refuse to intubate as it is medically futile
Consult the ethics committee and arrange a family meeting
Ask the daughter to prove the patient would want this

explanation.whyCorrect: Futility disputes: Ethics consult + Family meeting. Focus on patient's values.

whyWrong.A: Blindly following futile wishes violates ethical duty.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Futility disputes: Ethics consult + Family meeting. Time-limited trials can help.

vignette: A diagnostic test has sensitivity 95% and specificity 90% for a disease with 2% prevalence. If a patient tests positive, what is the approximate probability they actually have the disease?

explanation.clinicalPearl: PPV depends on PREVALENCE! Even excellent tests have low PPV when disease is rare.
